2. Report Structure & Format.section_structure: 5/5 — all key sections present (4/4), 33 headings total

3. Content Completeness.technology_evolution: 5/5 — Systematically traces major eras: pre-deep-learning (concatenative, HMM parametric), first-wave neural (WaveNet/Tacotron), non-autoregressive (FastSpeech), end-to-end (VITS), and 2021–2024 surge (diffusion, flows, codec-LMs like VALL-E), with key limitations and transitions described.
3. Content Completeness.architecture_analysis: 5/5 — Provides substantive technical analysis across multiple paradigms (autoregressive codec LMs: VALL-E/VALL-E 2; non-autoregressive: FastSpeech 2; diffusion: Diff-TTS/Grad-TTS/NaturalSpeech 2, distillation; flow-based: Glow-TTS/Flowtron; plus hybrid/flow-matching and controllable/style systems). Includes principles (codec tokenization, MAS, VAE+flows+GAN, score-matching, Schrödinger Bridges) and representative works well beyond the minimum four.
3. Content Completeness.technical_challenges: 5/5 — Covers core challenges explicitly requested: speed–quality tradeoff, expressiveness/prosodic diversity, cross-speaker/zero-shot generalization, cross-lingual/multilingual issues, and data-distribution robustness; also discusses mitigations (distillation, flow-matching, style conditioning, repetition-aware sampling) and adds ethics/safety as an additional relevant challenge.
3. Content Completeness.evaluation_framework: 4/5 — Strong subjective evaluation coverage (MOS/DMOS/CMOS, benchmark mention) and a broad set of objective metrics (WER/CER, SIM, MCD, UTMOS/MOSNet, F0 metrics, RTF). However, it omits several canonical perceptual/intelligibility metrics explicitly listed in the rubric (e.g., PESQ, STOI, FAD) and provides limited discussion of metric pros/cons beyond noting evaluation gaps.
3. Content Completeness.future_directions: 4/5 — Presents concrete, trend-grounded directions (RLHF, on-device real-time, multimodal/embodied TTS, universal multilingual models, scalable controllability, watermarking/provenance) with some time horizon claims, but lacks a clearer short/medium/long-term technical roadmap with dependencies, milestones, and risks to reach a top score.

4. Technical Depth.math_formulas: 0/5 — No explicit mathematical formulas (e.g., attention equations, ELBO, diffusion SDE/ODE definitions, flow Jacobians) or algorithm pseudocode are provided. There is also no computational complexity (Big-O) analysis for any architecture or inference scheme.
4. Technical Depth.industry_cases: 1/5 — Primarily surveys academic/research systems (VALL-E, VITS, Grad-TTS, NaturalSpeech 2, etc.) rather than real-world deployed industry products. It briefly alludes to deployment considerations (on-device, production workhorse) but provides no concrete case studies with architecture diagrams, deployment constraints, latency/throughput at scale, or operational metrics from commercial systems (e.g., Google/Microsoft/NVIDIA/iFlytek/Baidu).
4. Technical Depth.ethics_security: 2/5 — Includes a dedicated ethics/safety section and mentions deepfake risks, watermarking/provenance, and ASVspoof 5 as a benchmark. However, it lacks technical depth: no specific detection models/pipelines, no quantitative detection metrics (AUC/EER), no privacy/compliance analysis (GDPR/CCPA/DPIA), no concrete mitigation implementations (e.g., DP epsilons, federated learning), and no governance roadmap.
4. Technical Depth.quantitative_comparison: 3/5 — Provides some quantitative values and a metrics table (e.g., MOS 4.4, correlation ~0.85, RTF threshold, SIM > 0.85, WER/CER targets). However, it does not systematically compare multiple architectures side-by-side with consistent reported benchmarks (MOS/RTF/params) nor analyze root causes using comparable experimental evidence; most comparisons are qualitative.

5. Academic Standards.IEEE_format: 3/3 — standard IEEE numbered citation format detected
5. Academic Standards.terminology_consistency: 3/4 — Technical terminology (e.g., autoregressive/non-autoregressive, diffusion, flow-based, MOS/CMOS/DMOS, WER/CER) is largely accurate and used consistently, and most acronyms are expanded on first use (e.g., TTS, HMM, MOS). However, several placeholders/incomplete citations appear (e.g., "[ref]" for ProDiff, SC VALL-E, ControlSpeech, YourTTS mentions; "VALL-E X" cited as [ref] in text but [14] in references), and one cross-reference seems incorrect ("RLHF-based fine-tuning (§4.6)" while RLHF is discussed in §6.1). There are also minor bibliographic/numbering inconsistencies (e.g., Glow-TTS cited as [9] in text but listed as [13] in references), which weakens overall consistency.
5. Academic Standards.writing_style: 4/4 — The report maintains an objective, academic tone with clear logical organization, appropriate technical specificity, and minimal colloquial phrasing. Third-person perspective is consistently used; passive voice is used where appropriate but not overused. Definitions and comparisons are presented rigorously, and sections flow coherently from background to challenges, evaluation, and future directions.

eval_report.comment: Passing: Report framework is basically established, but content depth, academic standards, or data analysis have notable deficiencies.
